problems.problem: Need production-ready sandboxing solution for untrusted code execution
problems.description: In the post "llm predictions for 2026 shared with oxide and friends," Simon repeatedly emphasizes the urgent need to solve sandboxing to safely run untrusted code, particularly in the context of LLM-generated code and coding agents. He states this is "one of the most important problems to solve in 2026" and links to a comprehensive guide on sandboxing approaches, indicating active research into solutions.
evidence: I think this year is the year we're going to solve sandboxing. I want to run code other people have written on my computing devices without it destroying my computing devices if it's malicious or has bugs.
It's crazy that it's 2026 and I still `pip install` random code and then execute it in a way that it can steal all of my data and delete all my files.
I don't want to run a piece of code on any of my devices that somebody else wrote outside of sandbox ever again.
I think using the right sandboxes to safely run untrusted code is one of the most important problems to solve in 2026. This guide is an invaluable starting point.
problems.email_open_reasoning: Simon explicitly states this is one of the most important problems to solve in 2026 and shows urgency ('It's crazy that it's 2026...'). He's actively researching solutions (linking to detailed technical guides) and the problem affects his daily workflow with coding agents. He'd likely engage with concrete sandboxing solutions or implementation approaches.
problems.persistence_reasoning: Simon predicts sandboxing will be solved within 1 year (2026), indicating he expects this to be an active problem throughout the year. The problem is structural to his AI-assisted coding workflow and won't resolve until practical solutions are implemented and adopted.
problems.source_post: llm predictions for 2026 shared with oxide and friends

problems.problem: Preventing security disasters with coding agents running with elevated privileges
problems.description: Simon predicts a 'Challenger disaster' for coding agent security in 2026, acknowledging he and others are running coding agents 'practically as root' without proper safeguards. He references Johann Rehberger's essay on 'Normalization of Deviance in AI' and expresses concern about the industry operating unsafely because 'nothing bad has happened yet.'
evidence: I think we're due a Challenger disaster with respect to coding agent security [...] I think so many people, myself included, are running these coding agents practically as root, right? We're letting them do all of this stuff. And every time I do it, my computer doesn't get wiped. I'm like, 'oh, it's fine'.

The Normalization of Deviance describes the phenomenon where people and organizations get used to operating in an unsafe manner because nothing bad has happened to them yet, which can result in enormous problems
Every six months I predict that a headline-grabbing prompt injection attack is coming soon, and every six months it doesn't happen. This is my most recent version of that prediction!
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is a real concern Simon has articulated publicly and repeatedly, but it's more of a prediction/warning than an active problem he's trying to solve himself. He acknowledges the risk but continues current practices. He'd read an email about this carefully, especially if it offered concrete mitigation strategies, but it's not blocking his work today.
problems.persistence_reasoning: Simon gives this a 1-year timeline for when disaster might strike. The problem will remain relevant until either a major security incident occurs or the industry develops better practices. Somewhat related to the sandboxing problem but focused on organizational/behavioral change rather than technical solutions.
problems.source_post: llm predictions for 2026 shared with oxide and friends

problems.problem: Datasette instances getting hammered by bad bots with facet crawling
problems.description: In the post "vibe engineering", simonw notes that two of his public Datasette instances were "getting hammered with misbehaving bot traffic" on 2025-10-06. The main problem was poorly written crawlers visiting different variants of facet pages over and over. He had to scale up Fly instances temporarily and then disable facets entirely with `--setting allow_facet off` to stop paying extra for bot traffic.
evidence: Two of my public Datasette instances - for my TILs and my blog's backup mirror - were getting *hammered* with misbehaving bot traffic today.

The log files showed the main problem was facets: Datasette provides these by default on the table page, but they can be combined in ways that keep poorly written crawlers busy visiting different variants of the same page over and over again.
Scaling them up to more Fly instances got them running again but I'd rather not pay extra just so bots can crawl me harder.
So I turned those off. I'm now running those instances with `--setting allow_facet off`
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is a real technical problem that simonw encountered and solved, but he's already implemented a workaround (disabling facets). While he might be interested in better solutions, the immediate issue is resolved and this appears to be more of a known operational challenge than an active blocker needing external help.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is an ongoing operational issue with public Datasette instances and bot traffic. While he's implemented a temporary fix, better bot detection/mitigation would remain relevant as long as he runs these public instances, which appears to be an ongoing commitment.
